questions.question: In 1895, Wilhelm Roentgen noticed a fluorescent screen glowing across the room while operating a covered cathode-ray tube. What property of the unknown rays did this observation directly demonstrate?
options: They could penetrate opaque material and excite a screen at a distance
They were deflected by magnetic fields
They consisted of charged particles
They could be focused by glass lenses
questions.explanation: The tube was sealed in black cardboard, yet the screen glowed - the rays penetrated the opaque covering and traveled across the room. Penetration of opaque matter is the property radiography is built on.

questions.question: Becquerel's uranium salts and the Curies' radium emit radiation in a fundamentally different way than an X-ray tube. The key practical difference for the radiographer is that an isotope source:
options: Emits no radiation until it is electrically energized
Emits radiation continuously and can only be shielded, never switched off
Produces radiation only when heated
Stops emitting as soon as it leaves the reactor
questions.explanation: Radioactive decay is spontaneous and continuous. An X-ray machine stops emitting when de-energized; a gamma source never stops, which drives the entire source-handling safety discipline.

questions.question: A dense tungsten inclusion and a gas pore both sit inside the same weld. On the radiograph they appear, respectively:
options: Both darker than the surrounding weld
Tungsten lighter (more absorption), pore darker (less absorption)
Tungsten darker (less absorption), pore lighter (more absorption)
Both invisible unless the film is overexposed
questions.explanation: Differential absorption: high-density, high-Z tungsten absorbs more of the beam, so less radiation reaches the film and the spot prints light. The pore is missing metal, passes more radiation, and prints dark.

questions.question: Why does a radiographer survey with a calibrated meter after every exposure instead of relying on the camera's crank position?
options: Regulations require the meter only for the first shot of the day
Human senses cannot detect radiation and mechanical indicators can fail, so the meter is the only reliable proof the source is shielded
The meter measures the activity of the source in curies
Surveying speeds up film processing
questions.explanation: A disconnected or stuck source gives no sensory warning whatsoever. Indicators and cranks can lie; the survey meter reading on approach is the only trustworthy evidence the source is back in its shield.

questions.question: Simplify: 10⁶ ÷ 10²
options: 10¹²
questions.explanation: Dividing powers of the same base subtracts the exponents: 10⁶ ÷ 10² = 10⁶⁻² = 10⁴.

questions.question: A 240 Ci iridium-192 source decays for 222 days. With a half-life of about 74 days, what is its activity?
options: 120 Ci
60 Ci
30 Ci
80 Ci

questions.question: A survey meter reads 36 mR/h at 6 ft from an exposed source. At what distance does the field fall to 4 mR/h?
options: 54 ft
18 ft
12 ft
9 ft
questions.explanation: d₂ = d₁ × √(I₁/I₂) = 6 × √(36/4) = 6 × 3 = 18 ft. Solving the inverse square law for distance always ends in a square root.

questions.question: Which list orders the atomic particles from lightest to heaviest?
options: Proton, neutron, electron
Neutron, electron, proton
Electron, proton, neutron
Electron, neutron, proton
questions.explanation: The electron is about 1/1836 of a proton's mass; the neutron is very slightly heavier than the proton. So electron < proton < neutron.

questions.question: Two oxygen atoms bond to form O₂. This substance is best classified as:
options: A compound, because bonding always creates a new substance
A molecule of an element, because both atoms are the same element
A mixture of two gases
An ion, because the atoms share charge
questions.explanation: A molecule is two or more bonded atoms; it is only a compound when the atoms are different elements. O₂ is still simply oxygen.

questions.question: Cobalt-59 and cobalt-60 differ by:
options: One proton, making them different elements
One neutron - same element, but Co-60 is an unstable radioisotope while Co-59 is stable
One electron, making Co-60 a positive ion
Nothing - they are two names for the same nuclide
questions.explanation: Both have 27 protons (cobalt); Co-60 carries one extra neutron gained by activation in a reactor. That extra neutron destabilizes the nucleus, and its decay supplies the gamma rays used in thick-section radiography.
